scope.dataset: JDsearch official source plus verified Kaggle mirror

source_and_label_integrity.wrong_history_context_repair: One of 2,000 wrong-user assignments exceeded the only-second first-sequence budget. A label-free tokenizer guard dropped only that assignment's oldest effective event; the other 1,999 effective inputs were unchanged.

model_adequacy.adequacy_conclusion: QC is nontrivial and significantly above BM25; FULL-true exceeds QC and the recent-behavior control overall, while repeat requests provide a strong history-use positive control.

strict_nonrepeat.interpretation: True history is useful relative to the damaged FULL-null path, but its gain is smaller than the base capability lost during joint history training, so strict-nonrepeat ranking does not beat QC.

repeat.interpretation: Exact recurrence produces enough history utility to mask and overwhelm the same base erosion.

serialization_factorial_control.conclusion: The empty-history marker has only a small, statistically unstable effect. The large base loss persists under both serializations and is therefore localized primarily to the jointly history-trained weights rather than the marker format.

interpretation.primary: JDsearch does not replicate literal chance direction or zero history utility. It replicates the more defensible bottleneck: nonrepeat history creates almost universal score motion but converts only a small fraction of fixed response budget into correct ranking gains, far below recurrence.
interpretation.accounting: A positive true-over-null number is misleading by itself. On strict-nonrepeat requests it measures partial recovery from a base path damaged by history training, not improvement over an independently trained query-candidate ranker.
interpretation.positive_control: The same model converts recurrence into much stronger direction, history utility, and net gain, ruling out inactivity or generic inability to read history.
interpretation.provenance_boundary: True history beats the predominantly global wrong-user control on strict-nonrepeat, but the same-query other-user subset is underpowered and does not establish query-matched user specificity.
interpretation.semantic_boundary: JDsearch uses anonymized terms. It supports a functional ranking and credit-allocation law, not a pretrained-language semantic mechanism.

unsupported: Strict-nonrepeat JDsearch direction is indistinguishable from chance.
The empty-history text marker explains the base degradation.
Same-query user provenance is established on JDsearch strict-nonrepeat.
The observed tradeoff is already proven to require a new Transformer architecture.
next_action: Synthesize KuaiSearch, Amazon-C4 and JDsearch into the narrow controlled-composition motivation. Keep architecture locked until a frozen Failure Card additionally establishes recoverability and rules out a simple standard objective or training repair.
